coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production from Co-MOF/CN by nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: A novel composite photocatalyst for photocatalytic decomposition of water for hydrogen evolution was successfully synthesized by in-situ growth of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) nanoparticles on the surface of sheet cobalt-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN). The structure and properties of the obtained catalysts were systematically analyzed. NSCQDs effectively broaden the absorption of Co-MOF and CN in the visible region. The new composite photocatalyst has high hydrogen production activity and the hydrogen production rate reaches 6254 μmol/(g·h) at pH = 9. At the same time, NSCQDs synergy Co-MOF/CN composites have good stability. After four cycles of hydrogen production, the performance remains relatively stable. The transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot experimental results further demonstrate the improvement of carrier separation efficiency in composite catalysts. The semiconductor type (n-type semiconductor) of the single-phase catalyst was determined by the Mott–Schottky test, and the band structure was analyzed. The conductive and valence bands of CN are −0.99 and 1.72 eV, respectively, and the conduction and valence bands of Co-MOF are −1.85 and 1.33 eV, respectively. The mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction can be inferred, that is, Z-type heterojunction is formed between CN and Co-MOF, and NSCQDs was used as cocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production from Co-MOF/CN by nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composite photocatalyst for photocatalytic decomposition of water for hydrogen evolution was successfully synthesized by in-situ growth of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) nanoparticles on the surface of sheet cobalt-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN). The structure and properties of the obtained catalysts were systematically analyzed. NSCQDs effectively broaden the absorption of Co-MOF and CN in the visible region. The new composite photocatalyst has high hydrogen production activity and the hydrogen production rate reaches 6254 μmol/(g·h) at pH = 9. At the same time, NSCQDs synergy Co-MOF/CN composites have good stability. After four cycles of hydrogen production, the performance remains relatively stable. The transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot experimental results further demonstrate the improvement of carrier separation efficiency in composite catalysts. The semiconductor type (n-type semiconductor) of the single-phase catalyst was determined by the Mott–Schottky test, and the band structure was analyzed. The conductive and valence bands of CN are −0.99 and 1.72 eV, respectively, and the conduction and valence bands of Co-MOF are −1.85 and 1.33 eV, respectively. The mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction can be inferred, that is, Z-type heterojunction is formed between CN and Co-MOF, and NSCQDs was used as cocatalyst.

NSCQDs improve charge transfer and increase carrier separation efficiency. NSCQDs significantly enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of CN/Co-MOF Z-type heterojunction materials.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing problems of energy consumption and environmental pollution, it is urgently necessary to find clean and sustainable energy. 1 Now, photocatalytic technology has been widely studied in solving the energy crisis and controlling ecological pollution. The project uses light-driven decomposition of water to produce hydrogen, which could replace fossil fuels in the future. Therefore, hydrogen is the energy carrier to solve the problem of energy consumption and environmental pollution. 2–4 In recent years, numerous semiconductors have been used in the field of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. 5–8 However, even in photosensitizer systems, most semiconductors have low hydrogen evolution activity without auxiliary catalysts. 9 Therefore, the auxiliary catalysts play an important role in photocatalytic hydrogen production, especially in the dye-sensitized system.

The metal organic framework (MOF) is a new type of porous material, which is formed by the ordered self-assembly of organic ligands and metal ions. It has the unique advantages of adjustable porosity, various metal centers, porous structure, large specific surface area, and strong synergy. 10
,
11 In most studies, the single original MOF has a very low collection capacity for solar light irradiation, and the catalytic efficiency for solar fuel production is also very low. 12 To circumvent the limitations above of MOF as a single-component catalyst, it is feasible to combine other materials with MOF to prepare new composites. Liu et al. 13 found that the hexagonal prism-shaped Co-MOF with Hg-C3N4 had the best activity, and the best hydrogen evolution activity reached 1033 μmol/(g·h). Ejsmont et al. 14 used Co-MOF modified in situ with graphene oxide for photocatalytic hydrogen production, and the best hydrogen evolution activity reached 14.5 mmol/(g·h).

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN for short) has been extensively studied due to its excellent optical properties and low cost. However, the photocatalytic decomposition of water using a single CN has very low hydrogen production activity. Among the many modification schemes, the construction of heterojunctions, combined with the use of cocatalysts, and the use of organic dye sensitization can significantly improve the hydrogen production activity of g-C3N4. Coupling g-C3N4 with other semiconductors to form heterojunctions is an effective way to improve activity. 15–18 Jiang et al. 19 prepared a new photocatalyst using g-C3N4, and constructed a sulfur-doped g-C3N4/g-C3N4-based homogeneous stepped S-type heterojunction. The photocatalytic activity of the optimal sample was up to 5548.1 μmol/(g·h), and has good stability. Jiao et al. 20 successfully synthesized novel MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) modified g-C3N4 nanosheets/N doped carbon dots (NCDs) heterojunction photocatalysts. The ternary catalyst g-C3N4/NCDs/MoS2 photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the optimal catalyst is as high as 212.41 μmol/(g·h). Deng et al. 21 synthesized Ni/porous g-C3N4 via two steps of high-temperature thermal polymerization and photodeposition. Using nickel nanoparticles as cocatalysts, the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst was significantly enhanced, up to 1273.58 μmol/(g·h).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon materials with sp2 hybrid structure. Due to their nontoxic, ultraviolet-visible absorption, photoluminescence, light-induced electron transfer, and up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties, CQDs have been widely used in many fields, based on these advantages, carbon quantum dots are often used for photocatalytic hydrogen production from water decomposition. 22
,
23 Zhao et al. 24 prepared CQDs by the hydrothermal method using citrate as a carbon source, and combined it with TiO2 to prepare CQDs/TiO2 for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution with the best activity of 2.43 mmol/(g·h). Wang et al. 25 prepared a novel CQDs hybrid CdIn2S4 (CQDs/CIS) heterostructure by using biomass aloe as carbon source and low temperature carbonization at 200 °C to prepare CQD. The CQDs/CIS-3 heterostructure hydrogen evolution activity is as high as 956.79 μmol/(g·h) compared to 126.35 μmol/(g·h) for pure CdIn2S4. On the contrary, the hydrogen evolution activity of CQDs/CIS-3 increased 7.57 times. Ou-Yang et al. 26 used citric acid and ethylenediamine as carbon sources to synthesize CQDs by hydrothermal heating at 200 °C for 5 h. The prepared CQDs was introduced into oxygen-doped graphite carbon nitride (CNO). When CNO was added with 1.0 wt% CQDs, it showed the best photocatalytic activity, and the hydrogen production activity reached 316.6 μmol/(g·h) which was 2.1 times higher than that of a single CNO. At present, many research results show that CQDs has excellent performance in photocatalytic hydrogen production as a cocatalyst.

The CQDs features photosensitization, up-conversion effect, small size, and unique characteristics of electron capture. Coupling of CQDs with other catalysts improves visible light utilization and improves charge transfer. 27 CQDs can also effectively reduce the recombination of light-excited charge carriers, improve the efficiency of carrier separation, and promote the photocatalyst in the visible light response. 28 To optimize the properties of carbon quantum dots, it is often necessary to modify the carbon quantum dots, of which N, S doping is the most common. CQDs doped with nitrogen sources improves electron transfer and broadens the visible light absorption range, 29 N doping can also improve fluorescence quantum yields by introducing new surface states, and S doping can optimize the electronic structure of carbon quantum dots. N and S co-doping is more effective, and the introduction of S elements can enhance the induction effect of N elements in N-doped carbon quantum dots through a synergistic effect and introduce more active centers. 30
,
31

The lignite is rich in reserves and inexpensive in price. As for its structure, the lignite has a low degree of degradation and a high volatile content, and its disordered and loose structure is easy to produce a chemical reaction, it is an excellent carbon source for preparing carbon quantum dots. 32 In this study, lignite was used as the carbon source, and a small amount of urea was introduced as the nitrogen source, which can realize the nitrogen doping of carbon quantum dots. With high sulfur coal as a carbon source, sulfur self-doping can be realized. With high sulfur coal and a small amount of urea, nitrogen and sulfur co-doping can be realized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doping coal-based carbon quantum dots can improve the charge transfer ability between carbon quantum dots and semiconductor catalysts. In order to overcome the problem that the photocatalytic decomposition of water by a single catalyst has a very low hydrogen production activity, this study will use MOF materials combined with graphite phase carbon nitride, plus carbon quantum dots as a cocatalyst. Because CQDs coupled with other catalysts can improve visible light utilization and carrier separation efficiency, effectively reduce the recombination of light-excited charge carriers, and improve charge transfer, this study focuses on the preparation of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots using a green and sustainable method. Under the premise of maintaining the structural stability and large specific surface area of Co-MOF/g-C3N4, the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped coal-based carbon quantum dots were combined with Co-MOF/g-C3N4 by an in-situ growth method to explore the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the composite under visible light.

The coal samples used in the experiment were Inner Mongolian Tongliao lignite (TL) and Yunnan Lianhuatang lignite (LHT). Urea (CO(NH2)2), dimethyl imidazole (C4H6N2), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), eosin EY (C20H6Br4Na2O5), triethanolamine (C6H15NO3), and anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were purchased from Aladdin. All reagents used were of analytical grade.

The steady-state/transient fluorescence test was performed using the FluoroMax-4, a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer from HoribaScientific. The UV-vis absorption spectrum was analyzed using the UV-vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950) of Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd. The scanning interval is 1 nm, the spectral bandwidth is 2.0 nm, and the response time is 1.0 s. Morphological observation was performed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Talos F200S) at 200 kV. ZETA potential was tested by a ZETA potential tester (LITESIZER 500), with temperature set at 25 °C and balance time 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and analysis of sulfur forms
ce:section.ce:para: The selected coal sample was crushed by a jaw crusher, and the coal sample smaller than 74 μm was obtained by screening, and then the coal sample was analyzed by proximate and ultimate analysis of coal samples and sulfur forms analysis, as shown in Table 1 .

2 g of coal powder and 60 mL of distilled water were moved to the high pressure reactor and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. At the end of heat preservation, after the reactor was cooled to room temperature, it was filtered and purified by dialysis. After freeze drying at −40 °C, TL and LHT quantum dot powders were obtained, which were labeled as CQDs and SCQDs, respectively. 2 g of coal powder and 0.02 g of urea were prepared under the same conditions as CQDs to obtain TL and LHT nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, which were labeled NCQDs and NSCQDs respectively.

Weigh 1.3 g of dimethyl imidazole (C4H6N2), dissolve in 8 mL of H2O, and record it as solution a. Weigh 0.58 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, dissolve it in 40 mL of H2O, and record it as solution b. Under the agitation of solution a, drop solution b gradually into solution a, and let agitation continue for 4 h, age overnight, filter, and wash the mixture many times. Finally, transfer it to an oven at 60 °C and dry for 12 h to obtain Co-MOF.

Using urea as the precursor, g-C3N4 was prepared by the thermal condensation method. Weigh 10 g of urea, place the urea in the corundum crucible, and cover the crucible lid. In the muffle furnace, the final temperature was set to 550 °C, the heating rate was 5 °C/min, and the heat preservation was 2 h. After cooling, a light yellow powder was obtained. The light yellow powder was moved into a beaker, deionized water was added, ultrasonically dispersed, filtered, washed with deionized water several times, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain g-C3N4 (CN for short).

For the preparation of the composite catalyst, 1.3 g of dimethyl imidazole (C4H6N2) was dissolved in 8 mL of H2O, and 0.58 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of H2O. While stirring, the cobalt nitrate hexahydrate solution was slowly dropped into the dimethyl imidazole solution and then NSCQDs, CN was dissolved in the above mixed solution, stirred for 4 h, filtered, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. By adding different amounts of NSCQDs, CN, marked as xNSCQDs/Co-MOF/yCN (x = 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 mg; y = 0.05, 0.075, 0.100, 0.125, 0.15, 0.175, 0.200 g) (Fig. 1 ).

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was carried out in a 65 mL quartz bottle using 10% triethanolamine (TEOA) aqueous solution as a sacrificial reagent for hydrogen production, with 10 mg of catalyst and 20 mg of eosin EY dispersed in 30 mL of TEOA. Replace the air in the hydrogen cylinder with nitrogen. The experiment was carried out in a 5 W LED lamp multichannel photocatalytic reaction system. The needle was injected every 1 h and 0.5 mL of gas was extracted with a syringe and injected into a gas chromatograph to analyze the amount of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. And under the same conditions, four cycles of stability tests were carried out.

The photoelectrochemical characteristics of different catalysts were measured by the CHI660E electrochemical workstation. Transient photocurrent-time response, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) were performed on the working electrode. In a standard three-electrode battery, the electrolyte was a Na2SO4 solution of 0.2 mol/L, the auxiliary electrode was a platinum plate and Hg/HgO was the reference electrode, the working electrode was ITO-conductive glass coated with a mixed photocatalyst solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization and composition analysis

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the XRD and FT-IR images. In Fig. 2(a), g-C3N4 at 13.1° and 27.3° can be labelled as crystal planes (100) and (002), where the crystal plane (100) corresponds to the triazine group in the g-C3N4 plane, and the crystal plane (002) corresponds to the accumulation between layers aromatic rings, which can be identified as g-C3N4. 33–35 Co-MOF was synthesized in situ at room temperature, and before Co-MOF is less than 30°, it has the same characteristic peaks as reported in the literature, which can indicate that Co-MOF has been successfully prepared. 36–38

Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD images of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF, 15NCQDs/Co-MOF, 15NSCQDs/CN, and 15NCQDs/CN. For CN, the addition of NSCQDs or NCQDs did not affect the position, intensity and shape of the respective diffraction peaks of Co-MOF and CN, indicating that the introduction of NSCQDs or NCQDs has no significant effect on the crystal structure of the sample.

Fig. 2(c) is a FT-IR diagram showing the infrared spectra of NSCQDs, Co-MOF, CN, 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. In Fig. 2(c) the broad peaks in the spectrum between 3000 and 3600 cm−1 are caused by the tensile vibration of N–H, 39 and 1637 cm−1 belongs to the stretching of the C(sp2)=N unit in the aromatic heptazine heterocycle. The absorption peak at 1562 cm−1 belongs to the CN heterocyclic stretching mode. 40 The absorption peak at 1246 cm−1 belongs to the C–NH–C unit, and the absorption peak at 1406 cm−1 belongs to the telescopic vibratory heptazine unit model, 41 and the absorption peak at 808 cm−1 belongs to the heptazine ring (C6N7) in the g-C3N4 the structure. 42 The absorption peak at 540 cm−1 in the infrared characterization results of NSCQDs is attributed to the S–S stretching vibration. 43 The main absorption peaks of NSCQDs, Co-MOF, and CN are all reflected in 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN, which further indicates the successful preparation of the composite catalyst.

Fig. 2(d) shows the infrared spectra of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF, 15NCQDs/Co-MOF, 15NSCQDs/CN, and 15NCQDs/CN. As far as the infrared spectrum of Co-MOF is concerned, the stretching vibration of C=O at 1567 cm−1, the stretching bending mode of imidazole ring at 600–1500 cm−1, and the stretching bending of Co–N at 425 cm−1 can explain that Co-MOF is a coordination polymer. The addition of NSCQDs or NCQDs did not significantly affect the position and shape of the absorption peaks of Co-MOF and CN. It shows that 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF have similar surface chemical states. 15NSCQDs/CN and 15NCQDs/CN also have similar surface chemical states.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM and TEM images. The SEM images were used to study the microstructure and size of the catalyst. From the SEM image of Co-MOF in Fig. 3(a) the general morphology of the prepared Co-MOF presents a flower-like structure, and from Fig. 3(b) a single petal presents a leaf shape. The monolithic Co-MOF is about 6 μm long, 2 μm wide and 0.5 μm thick. It is a 3D structure. Fig. 3(c) shows the SEM image of CN. From Fig. 3(c) the prepared CN has a three-dimensional block structure with uneven surface. Fig. 3(d) shows the SEM image of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. Since the composite catalyst was prepared by in-situ growth method, MOF material was grown on CN in-situ. Therefore, the formed composite catalyst still generally presents the morphology of CN, and the surface has a leaf-like Co-MOF. The morphology of the composite catalyst is different from that of CN. Due to the growth of Co-MOF material, the surface of the composite catalyst has formed some leaf-like folds and the surface of the folds is relatively smooth, as if there is a film. Fig. 3(e) shows the SCQDs TEM image. The particle size distribution was analyzed by Nano Measure software. The maximum particle size was 4.02 nm, the minimum particle size was 1.34 nm, and the average particle size was 2.37 nm. Fig. 3(f) is the TEM image of NSCQDs, the particle size analysis statistics show that the maximum particle size is 4.48 nm, the minimum particle size is 1.76 nm, the average particle size is 2.71 nm, by comparison, it can be seen that after doping with the N element, the average particle size of carbon dots has increased. Fig. 3(g) is the TEM diagram of the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN catalyst. In the Fig., it can be clearly seen that there are some bulk CN and leaf-shaped Co-MOFs in close contact, which can also explain the successful preparation of the new catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. Fig. 3(h) and (i) show the element mapping images of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. From the element mapping results, it can be seen that there are C, N, Co, and S elements in the catalyst, and these elements are evenly distributed. The S element in the composite catalyst comes from NSCQDs. The uniform distribution of the S element can strongly indicate that NSCQDs is successfully loaded onto the composite catalyst, which further illustrates the successful preparation of the catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN.

Specific surface area and pore structure are important indicators of catalyst performance. According to the BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) method, the test results in Fig. 4 are obtained. As shown in Fig. 4(a), it can be seen from the catalyst nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves that all samples show type IV isotherms and have H3 hysteresis loops, indicating that the material has a mesoporous structure. 44 The micropore size is 0–2 nm, and the mesopore size is 2–50 nm, as shown in Table 2 , the average pore sizes of Co-MOF, CN, Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN are 7.63, 15.43, 14.00, 15.16 nm, respectively, and the main pore size distribution is 0–50 nm, it shows that the composite is a mesoporous material. The specific surface area of Co-MOF/0.125CN is significantly lower than that of CN because of the introduction of a Co-MOF with a larger size and smaller specific surface area into the Co-MOF/0.125CN composite catalyst. The increase in the specific surface area of the composite catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN compared to Co-MOF/0.125CN is due to the large amount of NSCQDs attached to the surface of Co-MOF/0.125CN. For catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN, not only is the specific surface area increased compared to Co-MOF/0.125CN, the pore volume and average pore size are also increased. Larger specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore size can provide more reaction sites in the same reaction system, which is conducive to efficient hydrogen evolution.

In order to analyze the change in elemental composition and binding energy of the catalyst, the composite catalyst was tested by XPS. Fig. 5 shows the XPS spectrum of the sample, and Fig. 5(a) shows the full XPS spectrum of each sample. It can be seen that 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN is mainly composed of Co, O, N, C, and S elements. The sulfur in the catalyst comes from NSCQDs, due to the low doping amount of NSCQDs, the S element peak in the spectrum is not very obvious.

Fig. 5(b) shows the C 1s spectrum, where 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN have two peaks at 284.8 and 288.0 eV, corresponding to C–C and N–C=N. 45
,
46 The binding energies corresponding to the C–C and N–C=N peak positions of 15NSCQDs/0.125CN are 284.6 and 287.9 eV. The electron binding energies of the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN peaks shift toward higher binding energies than those of the 15NSCQDs/0.125CN peak.

Fig. 5(c) shows the fine spectrum of N 1s. The electron binding energies of each peak of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN are 398.5 eV corresponding to C–N=C, 399.6 eV corresponding to N–C3, 401.20 eV corresponding to C–NH. 46 For 15NSCQDs/0.125CN, 398.6 eV corresponds to C–N=C, 399.7 eV corresponds to N–C3, and 401.3 eV corresponds to C–NH. The electron binding energies of the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN peaks shift toward lower binding energies compared to the 15NSCQDs/0.125CN.

Fig. 5(d) shows the fine spectrum of Co 2p, with 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN at 781.5 and 796.8 eV, corresponding to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, 47 and satellite peaks at 787.0 and 803.27 eV. 48 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF has satellite peaks at 781.7 and 797.0 eV, corresponding to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, and at 787.2 and 803.47 eV. The electron binding energies of the peaks of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN appear to shift toward lower binding energies compared to 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF.

Fig. 5(e) shows the fine spectrum of O 1s. The O 1s peak may be from the oxygen adsorbed in the air. The O-peak electron binding energies of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN appear to shift toward lower binding energies compared to 15NSCQDs/0.125CN.

Fig. 5(f) shows the fine spectrum of S 2p. Peaks of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN at 161.39, 163.71 and 168.56 eV corresponding to S 2p1/2, S 2p3/2 and SO3 2−. 49–51 The S 2p1/2, S 2p3/2 and SO3 2− of 15NSCQDs/0.125CN and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF are at 161.23, 163.59 and 168.46 eV, respectively. Compared to 15NSCQDs/0.125CN and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF, 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN shows a shift in peak position toward higher binding energy.

In Fig. 5, spectra C, N and Co are from Co-MOF/CN composite catalyst, S is from NSCQDs, which can indicate that NSCQDs has been successfully loaded on Co-MOF/CN. In the XPS spectra of the above samples, in the C, N, Co, O, and S spectra, the binding energy of the composite sample was changed compared with that of a single sample (in the case of simultaneous introduction of carbon quantum dots). The reason is that there is a strong coupling between CN and Co-MOF and NSCQDs, and the electron cloud density of the outer layer of C, N, Co, O, and S atoms has changed. It also shows the successful preparation of the composite catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the hydrogen production activity of different catalysts. From Fig. 6(a), it can be found that the catalytic activity of CN is very low and there is almost no hydrogen production. Co-MOF is active in the hydrogen production system, but the activity is not very high. On the basis of this, the next step is to try to compound the elementary catalyst according to a certain ratio to obtain 15NSCQDs/CN and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF. With the assistance of NSCQDs, the activity of the catalyst has been improved, but it has not yet reached the ideal state. By further adjusting the catalyst, 0.125 g of CN was added in the process of preparing Co-MOF, and NSCQDs was also added. The activity of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was found to increase significantly, and at the fifth hour, the hydrogen production activity was as high as 312.65 μmol. At the fifth hour, the hydrogen production activity of CN was 0.37 μmol, and the activity of Co-MOF was 23.77 μmol. It can be said that the performance of the composite catalyst compared with the single catalyst in the hydrogen production activity has improved significantly.

Fig. 6(b) shows the hydrogen production activity of the catalyst composite with different masses of CN. To explore the optimal amount of CN addition in the composite catalyst, the addition amounts were set at 0.050, 0.075, 0.100, 0.125, 0.150, 0.175, and 0.200 g, respectively. The optimal addition of CN was 0.125 g, which indicated that 15NSCQDs/CO-MOF/0.125CN had the highest hydrogen production activity.

Fig. 6(c) shows the hydrogen production activity of the catalyst composited with different CQDs. Different types of catalysts were prepared by adding different types of coal-based carbon quantum dots of the same quality during the catalyst preparation process. By comparison, it is found that the hydrogen production activity of NCQDs, SCQDs, and NSCQDs doped with composite catalyst is obviously better than that of composite catalyst doped with ordinary CQDs. In the fifth hour, the hydrogen production activity of 15CQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was 68.97 μmol, the hydrogen production activity of 15NCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was 81.21 μmol, the hydrogen production activity of 15SCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was 147.15 μmol, and the hydrogen production activity of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was 312.65 μmol. The hydrogen production activity of NSCQDs assisted Co-MOF/0.125CN was the highest, and the hydrogen production amount was 4.53 times that of the 15CQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN catalyst. It can be explained that the effect of doping NS on the hydrogen production of the CQDs-assisted catalyst is obvious.

Fig. 6(d) shows the hydrogen production activity of the catalyst composite with different masses of NSCQDs. On the basis of better hydrogen production activity of NSCQDs in assisting Co-MOF/0.125CN, we further studied the optimal input of NSCQDs in the preparation of the catalyst. The addition amounts were set to 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg, respectively. In terms of hydrogen production activity, the hydrogen production activity of the catalyst did not increase linearly with the increase of the addition amount of NSCQDs. The hydrogen production activity gradually increased with the addition of NSCQDs when the dosage was 0–15 mg, but the hydrogen production activity began to decrease after the dosage exceeded 15 mg. The reason is that NSCQDs can act as a bridge for the transmission of electrons in the catalyst, and can even store electrons, which is equivalent to an "electronic warehouse". NSCQDs in the injection of 15 mg are just to be able to evenly distribute on the surface of Co-MOF/0.125CN, more NSCQDs input will cause the accumulation of NSCQDs, is not conducive to the catalyst electron transport, and will hinder the exchange of electrons.

Fig. 6(e) shows the hydrogen production activity of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN under different pH conditions, and compares the effect of pH on the hydrogen production activity. Through the experimental results, it is found that the hydrogen evolution activity of the composite catalyst is very low under the conditions of strong acid and strong base. The pH of the best hydrogen production activity of the composite catalyst is 9, and the hydrogen evolution activity reaches 6254 μmol/(g·h). This is because in the strong alkali hydrogen production environment, the H+ supply capacity is insufficient, resulting in a decrease in the driving force for the reduction of H+ to H2, which reduces the hydrogen evolution activity. When the pH value is adjusted to pH = 7, a relatively high concentration of H+ will cause the solution to undergo protonation, thereby affecting the efficiency of the electron donor. 52

Fig. 6(f) shows the hydrogen production cycle experiment of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. From the results of cycle stability test, it can be seen that the hydrogen production activity of the composite catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN can still be similar to the first cycle after 20 h of continuous hydrogen production cycle experiment.

To study the transfer mechanism of photogenerated electrons, the steady-state fluorescence test and the fluorescence lifetime decay test were carried out on the catalyst. By fitting the fluorescence decay curve of EY and the fluorescence decay curve after introducing the corresponding catalyst into EY by Eq. (1), the transient fluorescence lifetime of the test sample is obtained by Eq. (2). Fig. 7 (a) shows the photoluminescence spectra of EY, Co-MOF, NSCQDs, CN, 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN. From the figure the fluorescence intensity of eosin EY aqueous solution is the highest, and the fluorescence intensity of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN is the lowest. The difference in fluorescence intensity can indicate the difference in the degree of electron-hole recombination. In the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN composite catalyst, the synergistic effect of Co-MOF, CN, and NSCQDs improves the efficiency of photogenerated electron and hole separation, so the fluorescence peak intensity is the lowest.

The fluorescence lifetime decay test was used to observe the kinetics of electron transfer. Fig. 7(b) shows the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra. Compared with EY, the fluorescence lifetimes of the catalysts Co-MOF, CN, and NSCQDs show different degrees of attenuation.

Table 3 shows the transient fluorescence lifetime of the measured samples. The average lifetime of EY was the longest (1.35 ns) compared with that of Co-MOF, CN and NSCQDs, while the average lifetime of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN was the shortest (0.14 ns). Shorter mean lifetimes mean faster decay kinetics. It shows that the composite catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN has higher charge separation efficiency, and the photogenerated electrons have a faster transfer rate. This performance improvement can be attributed to the introduction of NSCQDs, and the synergy between Co-MOF and CN, which together promote the transmission and transfer of photo-generated charges.

Fig. 8 shows the UV-vis diffuse reflectance and band gap diagrams of different catalysts. In order to analyze the optical properties and energy band of the catalyst, UV-vis diffuse reflectance analysis was carried out. Fig. 8(a) depicts the absorption spectra of different catalysts. By comparing the light absorption ranges corresponding to 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN and Co-MOF/0.125CN, it is found that the introduction of NSCQDs can increase the absorption range of light radiation. Fig. 8(b) depicts the absorption spectra of catalysts with different carbon quantum dots. It is found that the addition of NSCQDs has the best effect on broadening the light absorption range of the composite catalyst. Based on the Kubelka-Munk transformation function, the CN and Co-MOF bandgap graphs are obtained, and the bandgaps of CN and Co-MOF are approximately 2.71 and 3.18 eV, respectively.

In order to study the photoelectrochemical properties of CN, Co-MOF, NSCQDs and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN, the electrochemical performance of the prepared catalysts was tested. In order to study the charge transfer efficiency of the photocatalyst, the photocurrent response test was carried out. Fig. 9 (a) shows the transient photocurrent-time response, showing different photocurrent responses within 5 irradiation cycles. The composite catalyst 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN has the highest photocurrent. It shows that the interaction of NSCQDs with Co-MOF/0.125CN promotes the charge transfer efficiency of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN, and the composite catalyst has the highest electron transport efficiency, so the hydrogen production activity is the best. 53 In order to probe into the difficulty of charge transfer and transfer on the catalyst interface, the electrochemical impedance measurements were carried out for each catalyst. Fig. 9(b) is an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) image, finding that 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN has the smallest curvature radius, illustrating the highest charge transfer efficiency with excellent photoelectric capabilities. 54
,
55

Fig. 9(c) shows the linear sweep voltammetry curves (LSV). By testing CN, Co-MOF, NSCQDs and 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN, the results show that 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN has higher current density and lower hydrogen evolution overpotential than CN, Co-MOF and NSCQDs. In hydrogen production systems, a lower overpotential increases the rate at which H+ is reduced to H2. 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN has the highest electron transport efficiency and is an excellent photocatalyst. This may be due to the heterojunction of CN with Co-MOF and the synergistic effect of NSCQDs as a cocatalyst.

Fig.s 9(d) and (e) are Mott-Schottky graphs of CN and Co-MOF, showing tangent to the Mott-Schottky curve of CN and Co-MOF. It can be seen that the slope of a single CN and Co-MOF is greater than zero, indicating that both CN and Co-MOF belong to n-type semiconductors. 56
,
57 The Fermi level (E fb) of an n-type semiconductor is very close to that of a conduction band (CB). Therefore, the E fb values of single CN and Co-MOF are −1.03 and −1.89 eV, respectively. In n-type semiconductors, the E CB of the semiconductor is about 0.1 or 0.2 eV less than the E fb. 58
,
59 That is, compared with saturated calomel electrodes (SCE), the E CB values of CN and Co-MOF are about −1.23 and −2.09 eV. Under standard hydrogen electrode (NHE), the E CB values of CN and Co-MOF are −0.99 and −1.85 eV, respectively (E NHE = E SCE+0.241 eV). From CN and Co-MOF band gap diagrams in Fig. 8, the band gaps of CN and Co-MOF are about 2.71 and 3.18 eV, respectively. 60 From the formula (E VB = E CB + E g), the VB values of CN and Co-MOF are 1.72 and 1.33 eV respectively. Fig. 9(f) shows the Zeta potential of NSCQDs, Co-MOF and CN. The measurements provide a basis for the interaction between NSCQDs, Co-MOF and CN. It can be seen from Fig. 9(f) that the Zeta potentials of NSCQDs, Co-MOF, and CN are −28.5, −2.69, and 6.07 mV, respectively. It can be explained that NSCQDs has good dispersibility in aqueous solution, because the more negative the zeta potential, the better the dispersibility of the particles. 61 The greater the absolute value of the Zeta potential, the greater the affinity between positive and negative charges, and the higher the stability of the system. 62 NSCQDs and Co-MOF are both negative charges, and CN has a positive charge. There is electrostatic attraction between NSCQDs, Co-MOF and CN. The three can be tightly coupled together through electrostatic self-attraction, which is conducive to the transfer of electrons between the three. 63
,
64

Fig. 10 shows the mechanism of EY-sensitized NSCQDs enhancing the production of H2 by CN/Co-MOF heterojunction. Based on the energy band structure positional relationship between CN and Co-MOF, we speculate that the system is a Z-type heterojunction. 65
,
66 Under visible light, CN and Co-MOF produce photoelectron–hole pairs, and the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CN will be transferred and recombined toward the holes in the VB of Co-MOF. NSCQDs acts as a cocatalyst to facilitate light capture, promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and provide more reactive sites for CN/Co-MOF. It can be used as an electron acceptor to store electrons, which is equivalent to an "electron bin", and can also act as a bridge for electron transport in the catalyst to transport electrons. NSCQDs acts as an electron conduction bridge, which shortens the electron transfer path for CN and Co-MOF, promotes the transmission of electrons, and accelerates the migration of photo-generated carriers. Part of the electrons on the CB of CN are transferred to NSCQDs to drive the surface reaction, and the electrons are reduced on the surface of NSCQDs to produce hydrogen. At the same time, the holes produced by the light on the VB of Co-MOF have strong oxidizing ability and participate in the oxidation of TEOA as a hole scavenger. 67 The dye molecule EY is adsorbed on the surface of NSCQDs and CN/Co-MOF, and EY is converted to a single excited state EY1∗ after light excitation, and then to a more stable triple excited state EY3∗. 68 TEOA acts as an electron donor to reduce and quench EY3∗ to EY– with strong reducing power. 69 The electrons of EY– are transferred to the active site surface of the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN catalyst for H2 precipitation on the conduction band by a reduction reaction. 70 At the same time, the dye molecules return to the ground state. 71 Because the 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN composite catalyst has excellent electron acceptance and dye adsorption, it effectively reduces the recombination of photo-generated charges. NSCQDs not only provides a large number of active sites in the hydrogen evolution process, but also acts as an electron conduction bridge, providing an electron transfer path for CN and Co-MOF, and promoting the transmission of electrons. In turn, the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN is improved.

NSCQDs was successfully and greenly prepared by hydrothermal method, and NSCQDs was used to enhance CN/Co-MOF Z-type heterojunction materials for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Compared with CN and Co-MOF, 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN composite samples have the highest H2 production activity with a rate of up to 6254 μmol/(g·h). The improvement of photocatalytic performance can be attributed to the following two aspects. On the one hand, the Z-type heterojunction formed by CN/Co-MOF can effectively suppress the recombination rate of photo-generated charges. On the other hand, the cocatalyst NSCQDs can be used as an electron acceptor to store electrons, which is equivalent to an "electronic warehouse", and can also act as a bridge for electron transport in the catalyst to transport electrons, so NSCQDs can improve charge transfer and improve the separation efficiency of carriers. The high specific surface area and mesoporous structure of 15NSCQDs/Co-MOF/0.125CN provide more active sites for hydrogen evolution reaction.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
